# Regen Tribe Knowledge System

## Root Regen Tribe Knowledge System

System (metaphor for an integrated whole or network):

Ecosystem (metaphor for an interconnected network):

Regen Ecosystem (metaphor for system or network): Tribe builds the ecosystem that accelerates Regenerative Neighborhoods.

A Regenerative Neighborhood closes loops.
Water. Food. Energy. Waste.
It builds trust.
It builds shared memory.
It replicates.

The mission serves regenerative communities.
The mission serves the commons.
The mission serves the planet.

The mission is threefold.
Accelerate the development of Regenerative Neighborhoods.
Accelerate the development of community infrastructure.
Accelerate the development of knowledge commons.

The mission excludes extraction.
The mission excludes hierarchy.
The mission excludes rent.

Every system fits one of five levels.

Cell (metaphor for a basic unit or module):

Cell (metaphor for basic unit or module): Level 1 is the Cell.
One person.
One home.
One skill.
The smallest unit that can act.

Tissue (metaphor for a connected group or network):

Tissue (metaphor for connected group or network): Level 2 is the Tissue.
Three to twelve people.
A household or a core team.
Trust forms here.
Small groups share and decide.

Organ (metaphor for a functional subsystem or team):

Level 3 is the Organ.
Twelve to 200 people.
One neighborhood.
Shared infrastructure.
Local governance.
The scale where collective action works.

Level 4 is the System.
A bioregion.
Multiple neighborhoods.
Trade. Federation.
Distances that need transport or communication.

Body (metaphor for the complete collective or organism):

Level 5 is the Body.
The whole planetary network.
Knowledge commons.
Standards that let bioregions talk.
Shared infrastructure at global scale.

The five levels give every system a size and scope.

Every system spans four domains.

Domain A is Physical.
What you build.
How you build it.
What it costs.

Domain B is Social.
Who does what.
How decisions get made.
How trust grows.
How conflict gets resolved.

Domain C is Information.
What you know.
How you know it.
How memory persists.
How sensing happens.

Domain D is Exchange.
What moves between people and places.
Value. Currency. Energy. Knowledge.
How trade happens without extraction.

The four domains give every system a type.

The five levels and four domains form a grid.
Every project occupies one cell in this grid.
The work is to fill the grid with working systems.

Regenerative communities operate at five levels.
They operate across four domains.
They close four loops.
They build four forms of trust.

The loop principle governs physical infrastructure.
Output of one system becomes input of another.
Water loops. Food loops. Energy loops. Waste loops.

The trust principle governs social infrastructure.
Trust grows through repeated interaction.
Trust follows evidence over claims.

The memory principle governs information infrastructure.
Knowledge persists across time.
Knowledge connects across space.

The circulation principle governs exchange infrastructure.
Value circulates.
Value does not accumulate.
Value does not extract.

The mission requires four pillars.
Physical independence. Economic independence.
Governance independence. Social independence.

Physical independence means closed-loop systems.
Energy from sun. Water from rain. Food from soil.
Waste returns to soil. No external dependency.

Economic independence means no debt.
No rent. No interest.
Time banking. Gift economy.
Contribution measured. Extraction visible.

Governance independence means consent-based decision making.
No external authority. No hierarchy.
Roles guide. Roles do not command.

Social independence means high-trust networks.
Trust emerges from shared experience.
Trust deepens through shared risk.
Trust solidifies through shared creation.

Every system must resist four types of capture.

Capital capture means wealth leaving the community.
Prevention: circulation over compounding.
Surplus flows back in.
The commons retains surplus.

Narrative capture means attention serving outside interests.
Prevention: commons over platform.
Shared story stays local.
Shared story serves local purpose.

Identity capture means people sorted into scores.
Prevention: contribution over score.
What you do matters.
Who you know does not matter.

Governance capture means rules set by outsiders.
Prevention: exit-friendly design.
Leaving must be easy.
No lock-in.

Technology serves life.
Not the other way around.
Open tools. Community owned hardware.
No royalties. No lock-in.

The design method is constraint-based.
You build by constraining.
Not by adding.
The wall is the design.
The bounded space is the feature.

The architecture is compositional.
Small parts. Large behaviors.
Nodes compose into workflows.
Beliefs compose into knowledge.
Roles compose into governance.

Formation Coding Language (FCL) sends ideas through space as shapes that change over time.
FCL uses geometry.
Geometry is universal.
All intelligent systems understand geometry.

AI serves the mission when it is open.
AI serves extraction when it is closed.
OpenAI serves proprietary models.
Open models serve commons.
The choice of open models matters.

No debt.
No rent.
No interest.
Value flows because it can.
Contribution is visible.
Extraction is visible.
Both get named.

Start at ROOT.
Read  through  in order.
Follow your interest.
Each document ends with related document numbers.
Follow the numbers.

Build a water system.
Read Domain A at Level 3.
Then Domain C at Level 3.
Then Domain B at Level 3.

Design a communication system.
Read Domain C at Level 3.
Then Domain D at Level 3.
Then Domain C at Level 4.

Design an AI agent.
Read Domain C at Level 3.
Then Domain C at Level 5.
Then .

This is not a company.
There is no product.
This is not a philosophy.
There is no guru.
This is not a legal entity.
There is no CEO.

This is a set of open documents.
They describe systems that work.
They can be read by anyone.
They can be copied by anyone.
They can be changed by anyone who contributes.

## The Unit Physical Domain

The smallest unit is one person with one working skill.

One person with a welding mask and a grinder can make parts.
One person with a 3D printer can make enclosures.
One person with a microcontroller and a sensor can measure the world.

The threshold for contribution is this.
You can contribute when you can make one thing that works.

The threshold is not money.
The threshold is not credentials.
The threshold is capability.
Capability means one working thing.

Tools multiply skill.
One person with power tools equals ten people with hand tools.

The first step is always the same.
Make one thing. Test it. Fix it. Make it again.

A home workshop produces sensor enclosures.
A home workshop produces pipe fittings.
A home workshop produces brackets.
A home workshop produces molds.
A home workshop produces tools for others.

A basic workshop requires weeks to months of collective skilled effort.
The range depends on salvage versus new materials.
Tissue (metaphor for a connected group or network):

Tissue (metaphor for connected group or network): The tissue decides the target based on available labor.
Cost depends on new versus used.
Cost depends on specialization.
Start cheap. Upgrade as needed.

The time to first output ranges from one day to one week.
First output builds confidence.
First output reveals the next step.

No permission is needed.
No supplier is needed.
No license is needed.
Start where you are.

Steel covers most structural needs.
Plastic covers most enclosure needs.
Wood covers most formwork needs.
Concrete covers most foundation needs.
These four materials cover 90 percent of needs.

Learn one process per month.
Welding. Casting. 3D printing. Machining.
Start with what you have.
Move to what you need.

Community starts when one person shows another.
Teaching by making is the method.
The demo is the lesson.
Words are secondary.

002 010

## The Unit Information Domain

IoT stands for Internet of Things.
It means sensors connected to a network.
A sensor measures a physical quantity.
It sends the reading over the network.

A microcontroller runs at 240MHz.
Capitalism prices this at one hour of minimum wage labor. The component costs a few cents to manufacture. The difference is profit margin and rent.
It has WiFi.
It can run IoT software.

Capitalism prices this at one hour of minimum wage labor. The component costs less than one dollar to produce. The rest is extraction.
It measures apparent temperature.
It measures relative humidity.

The microcontroller reads the sensor.
It stores the reading.
It sends the reading over WiFi.
System (metaphor for an integrated whole or network):

This is the smallest possible sensing system.

MQTT stands for Message Queuing Telemetry Transport.
It is a sensor network protocol.
The microcontroller publishes to a topic.
A server subscribes to the topic.
The topic name tells you what the reading means.

One reading per hour is enough for most purposes.
That is 24 readings per day.
24 readings track daily cycles.
24 readings fit in memory.

Store readings in a time-series database.
A time-series database holds readings with timestamps.
It answers what was the value at time X.
It answers how did the value change over time.

A hidden variable is something you cannot measure directly.
True temperature is a hidden variable.
A sensor measures apparent temperature.
The difference is sensor error.
Bayesian inference separates the two.

Bayesian inference updates belief when data arrives.
Prior belief is what you thought before the reading.
Posterior belief is what you think after.
Posterior is proportional to Prior times Likelihood.
Likelihood measures how likely the reading is given current belief.

weather_state is one shared hidden variable.
All sensors contribute to it.
It represents the real state of the environment.
It encodes temperature. Humidity. Rainfall. Solar radiation.

The model is this.
Sensor reading equals true value plus noise.
Noise has a distribution.
Bayesian inference finds the distribution of the true value.

This math applies to temperature.
It applies to humidity.
It applies to water level.
It applies to soil moisture.
It applies to battery voltage.
Learn it once. Use it everywhere.

Store beliefs in a knowledge graph database.
Each belief has a mean. Each belief has a variance.
Variance tells you confidence.
Low variance means high confidence.
High variance means low confidence.

More readings reduce variance.
More sensors reduce variance faster.
The goal is to reduce variance over time.

Tissue (metaphor for a connected group or network):

Cell (metaphor for a basic unit or module):

Cell (metaphor for basic unit or module): Tissue (metaphor for connected group or network): The cell information layer feeds the tissue layer.
When two people share sensors they share weather_state.
This is how collective sensing begins.
One reading at a time.

001 011 021

## The Group Physical Domain

Three people with shared tools build more than ten people with no tools.

Tissue (metaphor for a connected group or network):

Tissue (metaphor for connected group or network): A shared workshop is the first physical infrastructure of a tissue.
The workshop holds a welder. It holds a drill press.
It holds an angle grinder. It holds a 3D printer. It holds hand tools.

Capitalism prices this at what a skilled person earns in one to four months of full-time work.
A community builds it for the effort of one season of shared labor.
Buy used. Fix it yourself.
Learn repair as you go.

What one person fabricates in a week of precision work. A mill for water systems.
What one person fabricates in a week of precision work. It makes parts for sensors.

The tissue workshop produces sensor mounts.
It produces pipe fittings. It produces enclosures. It produces replacement parts.

Production for members works like this.
The tissue makes things for its members.
Members contribute time credits.

Organ (metaphor for a functional subsystem or team):

The tissue makes things for the organ.
The organ pays in goods or labor.

No money enters the picture at this level.
Time and goods only.
Money introduces extraction.

System (metaphor for an integrated whole or network):

Maintenance is part of the system.
Machines break. Someone learns to fix each machine.
This person becomes the maintenance lead.

Tool library model works like this.
The tissue owns tools. Members borrow them. Members return them clean.
This prevents the common failure.
Each household buys What one skilled person makes in a week of focused effort. Each uses it once.

Training goes in steps.
Operator. Builder. Designer. Teacher.
Certification means you can teach the skill to someone else.
This is the only certification that matters. Not paper. Demonstrated skill.

Safety is the first lesson.
Every machine can hurt you.
First aid kit is mandatory.
Fire extinguisher is mandatory.
Eye wash is mandatory.

The tissue maintains the land it sits on.
Fence. Access road. Water. Power.
These tasks go into the time banking system.

The tissue physical layer feeds the organ physical layer.
The organ needs what the tissue makes.
The tissue needs what the organ provides.
This is the beginning of the exchange network.

001 011 020

## The Group Social Domain

Tissue (metaphor for a connected group or network):

Tissue (metaphor for connected group or network): A tissue is a small group.
Three to twelve people.
A household or a core team.

Trust is the foundation.
Everything else builds on it.

Trust grows through repeated interaction.
You do what you say. Others see.
Keeping small promises builds trust.
Breaking small promises erodes trust.

The first trust act is keeping a small promise.
Nothing more.
Trust builds slowly.
Trust breaks quickly.

Roles emerge from what people actually do.
Roles do not emerge from what people say they will do.
Assign roles by observation.
Watch who does what well.
Ask them to do more of it.

Consent is the decision method.
A decision passes when no one blocks it.

Blocking is serious.
Blocking means the proposed action would harm the blocker.
Blocking requires a proposal everyone can live with.
Not everyone loves it. Just lives with it.

Consent is not agreement.
Agreement means yes.
Consent means no blocking.

Time banking measures contribution.
One hour equals one time credit.
All work counts. Cooking. Cleaning. Repair. Teaching. Listening.

Time credits do not earn interest.
This is the rule. No exceptions.

Time credits have a floor.
You never go below zero.
The community tops you up.

Time credits have a ceiling.
You cannot accumulate beyond 200 hours per year.
The ceiling prevents hoarding. The floor prevents poverty.

Conflict is normal.
It is a signal.
It means something needs attention.

The conflict process: name the issue. Hear all sides. Propose. Test.

If unresolved get a third party.
Someone outside the group.
They do not decide. They help the group decide.

Meetings have a format.
Check-in. Agenda. Discussion. Decision. Check-out.
Check-in means one sentence on how you feel.
Check-out means one sentence on what you got.

This format takes 15 minutes.
It prevents most meeting problems.

Decision logs are written.
Who. When. Why.
This is memory.

Memory prevents the same arguments again and again.
When someone leaves the group keeps functioning.
Decision logs remain.
This is the key advantage over informal groups.

A tissue has a guide. Not a leader.
The guide holds the process.
The guide does not make decisions.
The guide makes sure decisions get made well.

010 022 002

## The Organ Physical Domain

Organ (metaphor for a functional subsystem or team):

An organ is one neighborhood.
Twelve to 200 people sharing infrastructure.

The standard stack has five parts.
Water. Food. Energy. Waste. Shelter.

Each part closes a loop.
Output of one becomes input of another.
Nothing leaves. Everything cycles.

Water closes its loop this way.
Rain falls on the roof. It flows to the tank.
It is filtered. It is used.
Greywater from sinks goes to the garden.
The constructed wetland cleans the water.
Plants take the nutrients. Clean water returns.

Food closes its loop this way.
A food forest grows on the perimeter.
Aquaponics grows near the center.
Compost turns waste into soil.
Soil grows food. The loop closes.

A food forest has seven layers.
Canopy. Sub-canopy. Shrub. Herb. Ground cover. Root. Vine.

Aquaponics grows fish and plants together.
Fish waste feeds plants. Plants clean the water.
The fish provide protein. The plants provide vegetables.

Energy closes its loop this way.
Solar PV panels charge batteries during the day.
Batteries power the neighborhood at night.
Mesh routing sends power where it is needed.
No central grid required.
The neighborhood disconnects from the external grid.
This is the goal. Energy independence.

Waste closes its loop this way.
All organic waste goes to compost.
Worms process some of it. The rest becomes soil.
Greywater recycles. Blackwater processes separately.

Shelter closes its loop this way.
3D printed construction uses local soil.
No transport cost. No shipping emissions.
Passive design means the building heats and cools itself.
System (metaphor for an integrated whole or network):

No mechanical system needed.

Capitalism prices this at what a household earns in three to six months of labor.
A community builds it for what three households produce together in one season.
Construction time is one week per home.

The standard stack is not mandatory.
Each neighborhood adapts it.
What matters is the loop principle.
Every output becomes an input somewhere else.

A neighborhood that closes its loops is regenerative.
It gives more than it takes.

The organ physical layer is maintained by the organ social layer.
Decisions about infrastructure are made by the organ social layer.

010 022 023

## The Organ Information Domain

Organ (metaphor for a functional subsystem or team):

An organ is one neighborhood.
Twelve to 200 people.

A neighborhood sensor mesh covers the area.
Each home has one node. Each water tank has one node. Each garden has one node.

Nodes form a mesh network.
They talk to each other. They talk to a server.
The server runs a knowledge graph database.
It holds beliefs. It holds relations.

The server runs a time-series database.
It holds sensor readings. Each reading has a timestamp.
It answers what was the value at time X.

One shared latent variable connects all sensors.
This variable represents real environment state.
Weather. Humidity. Soil moisture. Water level. Battery voltage.

Bayesian inference updates the variable.
New readings narrow the probability distribution.
More sensors narrow it faster.
The model captures correlations.

Rain raises the water tank.
Rain raises the soil moisture.
High temperature increases evaporation.
High humidity slows evaporation.
System (metaphor for an integrated whole or network):

The system sees these connections.

A digital twin mirrors the physical neighborhood.
It updates as sensors update.
It runs predictions. It shows what might go wrong.

Capitalism prices a small server at what one hour of skilled technical work costs.
The community builds it for the effort of less than one day of technical work.

Capitalism prices each sensor at one hour of minimum wage labor.
The component costs cents to manufacture.
The community builds each for the effort of less than one hour of assembly.

Capitalism prices a full neighborhood mesh at what a small technical team earns in one to four weeks.
The community installs it for the effort of one to two weeks of coordinated technical work.
002 020 023 040 053

## The Organ Social Domain

Organ (metaphor for a functional subsystem or team):

An organ is one neighborhood.
Twelve to 200 people.

Governance at this scale needs structure.
Not hierarchy. Structure.

The council holds the decisions.
Tissue (metaphor for a connected group or network):

Tissue (metaphor for connected group or network): Each tissue sends one representative.
The council meets once per week.

The council handles four things.
Infrastructure. Disputes. Planning. Resources.

Infrastructure decisions answer: what to build. How to maintain. Who pays.

Disputes means conflicts between tissues.
The council mediates. It does not judge.

Planning means the council sets the quarterly direction.
The tissues execute.

Resources means the council allocates shared tools and land and water and energy.

Consent still rules.
A decision passes when no council member blocks it.

Each council member can block on behalf of their tissue.
This is their job. Blocking is serious.

The council has a guide. Not a chair.
The guide holds the process.

Roles at the organ level: infrastructure lead. Food lead. Energy lead. Education lead.

Each role has a charter.
Purpose defines why. Scope defines what. Authority defines decisions. Accountability defines review.

The council reviews each role every quarter.
Accountability means show your work. Explain your decisions. Accept feedback.

Land is held in common.
No one person owns the land. The organ holds the title.

Buildings are owned by the builders. Or by the organ.
This is decided case by case.

When someone leaves their building can be bought by the organ. Or removed. Or passed to family.
No fixed rule. Case by case.

What matters: the land stays in the commons. The knowledge stays in the commons.

The commons is protected by the council.
The council is guided by consent.

Conflict at the organ level: two tissues disagree. The council mediates.
If mediation fails an external mediator helps. They do not decide. They help.

Communication: each tissue posts a weekly update. What they did. What they will do.

Decision logs are public. Anyone can read them.
This prevents power concentration. Hidden decisions enable capture.

Open records are the anti-capture mechanism.

011 020 023

## The Organ Exchange Domain

Organ (metaphor for a functional subsystem or team):

Exchange at the organ level means goods and services and time moving between people.

Time banking is the base unit.
One hour equals one time credit.
All work counts. Cooking. Cleaning. Repair. Teaching. Listening.

Time credits do not earn interest.
This is the rule. No exceptions.

Time credits have a floor.
You never go below zero. The community tops you up.

Time credits have a ceiling.
You cannot accumulate beyond 200 hours per year.
The ceiling prevents hoarding. The floor prevents poverty.

The organ runs a surplus store.
Members add what they grow. Members add what they make. Members add what they find.

Other members take from the surplus store.
They pay with time credits.

Surplus goes to neighboring organs.
What is not taken locally goes externally.

The council tracks exchanges.
It makes sure no zone always gives and never receives.

Balance is reviewed quarterly.
A zone that gives more gets recognition.
A zone that receives more gets a support plan.
No shame. Just help.

External trade follows the same rules.
External goods get priced in time credit equivalent.
Pricing reflects labor time plus materials.

No rent. No interest. No debt.
These are anti-extraction rules.

If someone needs something the organ provides it.
The person does not owe.

What matters is contribution.
What you give to the commons. Not what you hoard.

External money is tracked.
It does not become the base unit. Time credits stay primary.

011 022 020

## The Body Information Domain

Body (metaphor for the complete collective or organism):

The body is the whole planetary network.
All bioregions. All organs. All tissues.

Information at this scale needs standards.
Without standards nothing connects.

A standard is an agreement.
It says this format means this thing.

The knowledge commons holds standards.
It holds best practices. It holds failure reports.

Organ (metaphor for a functional subsystem or team):

Every organ contributes what it learns.
Every organ takes what others have learned.

The knowledge graph grows.
Each node is a concept. Each edge is a relation.

A concept has a name. A concept has a definition.
A concept has evidence. A concept has a confidence level. A concept has a source.

An edge has a from concept. An edge has a to concept.
An edge has a type. An edge has a strength of evidence.

The graph is queried.
A question comes in. The graph returns relevant concepts.

A decision support query example.
System (metaphor for an integrated whole or network):

What is the best water system for clay soil in a dry climate.
The graph answers with matching concepts. With connecting relations. With confidence levels.

Standards at the body level: data formats. Measurement units. Safety thresholds.

Data format standard uses JSON for sensor readings. Parquet for large datasets.

Measurement unit standard uses SI units.
Liters. Kilograms. Meters. Seconds. Celsius.

Safety threshold standard uses maximum contaminant levels.
These do not vary by region.

The knowledge commons is open.
Read by anyone. Write by verified contributors.

Each contribution is reviewed. Accuracy matters.

False information is flagged.
Repeated false information gets a contributor banned.

Old information gets flagged for review.
Standards evolve. Old numbers get replaced.

The commons runs on open source.
Code is open. Data is open.

No corporation owns the commons.
No government owns the commons.
The users own it.

The commons is funded by contribution.
Each organ contributes time or goods annually.

Governance falls to a council of bioregion representatives.
They set standards.

Standards changes go through proposal. Comment. Revision. Final vote.

The body sees patterns across bioregions.
It sees which water systems work in dry climates.
It sees which fail. It sees which soil methods work in clay. It sees which fail.

A desert organ learns from a tropical organ.
A cold organ learns from a warm organ.
The graph connects distant experiments.

The body runs on distributed architecture.
Each server holds a graph partition.
They sync with each other.

The body has no central brain.
Many nodes cooperate.
Query routing finds relevant nodes.

The body has memory.
It remembers what worked. It remembers what failed.
Old failures stay visible. They warn new experiments.

A bioregion starting a water project queries the graph.
It finds similar projects. It finds what worked. It finds what to avoid.
It saves time and resources.

The graph supports decisions. It does not make decisions.
People make decisions.

021 050 051 053 054

## Formation Coding Language

Formation Coding Language (FCL) sends ideas through space as shapes that change over time.

FCL does not send text. FCL does not send images.
FCL sends a shape that changes over time.
The shape is the message.

Normal communication depends on symbols.
Symbols must be agreed upon in advance.
Sender and receiver must share the symbol set.
If they do not, communication fails.

FCL does not use symbols.
FCL uses geometry.
Geometry is universal.
All intelligent systems understand geometry.
Two points make a line. Three points make a triangle.
A triangle means the same thing to any observer.

Sender and receiver share a knowledge graph.
The graph has nodes. The graph has edges.
Nodes are concepts. Edges are relations.
Both parties must have the same graph structure.
If they do not, the formation cannot decode.
This is the foundation.

A node becomes a point.
An edge becomes a vector.
The vector has direction. The vector has length.
Direction encodes the relation type.
Length encodes the relation strength.
The formation shows the graph structure in space.

The formation changes over time.
One shape becomes another shape.
The change encodes sequence.
The timing of pulses encodes priority.
Morphing between shapes encodes transition.
Time adds a second dimension of meaning.

A formation can describe other formations.
This enables recursion.
Recursion enables self-reference.
Self-reference enables reflection.
This layer makes FCL theoretically complete.

An autonomous aerial vehicle (AAV) carries LEDs.
It forms shapes in the sky.
Together they form a formation.
A formation sends a message. The message is the shape.

A bird flock is a natural substrate.
No permission needed. Lower precision. Lower bandwidth.
Starlings demonstrate this works.

A holographic projection is a volumetric display.
No physical vehicles required.
Limited by display technology.

A digital screen is 2D.
The formation loses a dimension. Some information is lost.

Start with a knowledge graph.
Choose a traversal path through the graph.
Map each node to a point.
Map each edge to a vector between points.
Add temporal sequence.
Encode as formation parameters.
Send on the chosen substrate.

Detect the formation. Reconstruct the points and vectors.
Map the geometry back to the knowledge graph.
Trace the traversal path.
Output the concept sequence.
Verify the graph structure matches.

FCL is not a display technology. The formation is not a picture.

FCL is not steganography. There is no hidden message. The shape is the message.

FCL is not morse code. No agreed symbol set. Geometry carries meaning.

FCL is not an image format. The meaning is in the graph traversal. Not in pixels.

051 052 021 000

## Affinity Mapping Engine And Field Of Trust

Affinity Mapping Engine stands for Affinity Mapping Engine.
Field of Trust stands for Field of Trust.
Both come from the Mythogen framework developed by Vic Desotelle.
It addresses community formation.
It addresses trust maintenance.
It addresses collective coherence.

Affinity means natural attraction to work.
Affinity means shared purpose without coercion.
Traditional management assigns tasks.
People receive orders.
They comply or leave.
This extracts compliance.
It does not build affinity.

Affinity mapping finds patterns in group affinity.
It finds who wants to work on what.
It finds who works well with whom.
It surfaces compatibility.
It surfaces resonance.
It does not assign.
It illuminates.

The engine listens to group signals. It does not extract data. Extraction violates trust. Extraction creates resistance. The engine observes. Observation without intrusion. Pattern recognition without coercion.

Signal types include language patterns.
Language patterns reveal interest.
Word choice reveals emotional state.
Signal types include gesture patterns.
Gesture patterns reveal energy.
Eye contact reveals connection.
Signal types include choice patterns.
Choice patterns reveal values.
Choice of partners reveals affinity.
Signal types include proximity patterns.
Proximity patterns reveal trust.
Who stands near whom.
Signal types include time allocation patterns.
Time allocation reveals priority.
Where time goes reveals what matters.

The engine weights signals by frequency. By intensity. By consistency. It produces an affinity map. The map shows cluster structure. The map shows strength of connection.

Affinity clusters form around shared work. Affinity clusters form around shared values. Affinity clusters form around complementary skills. Affinity clusters form around trust relationships.

The map guides role assignment. It does not dictate. It suggests. It illuminates. People choose their affinity match. Agency remains with the individual.

Trust emerges from repeated interaction.
Trust follows a formula.
Trust equals evidence divided by claims.
Evidence is what you do.
Claims are what you say.
High evidence over claims means high trust.
Low evidence over claims means low trust.
High claims over evidence means reputation inflation.

Trust is not binary. It is a ratio. The ratio shifts with every action. Every interaction updates the ratio. Trust is dynamic. Trust is measurable. Trust is comparable.

A group with high FOT coordinates without hierarchy. Coordination emerges from mutual evidence. People adjust based on observed behavior. No central authority needed. No enforcement needed.

A group with low FOT needs rules and enforcement.
Rules compensate for lack of trust.
Enforcement compensates for lack of evidence.
The group expends energy on control.
Energy not available for production.

The goal is to raise the FOT. Raise it by producing evidence. Consistent action over time. Keeping promises. Reduce it by overclaiming. Claims without action erode trust.

The FOT composite is the minimum of five indicators. This is the hologram principle. One weak indicator drags the whole field down. All five must be maintained.

Indicator 1 is reliability. Do you do what you say. Do you show up. Do you deliver.

Indicator 2 is competence. Can you do what you claim. Do you have the skill. The knowledge.

Indicator 3 is integrity. Do you say what you mean. Do you mean what you say.

Indicator 4 is consistency. Do you behave the same over time. Across contexts. Can others predict your behavior.

Indicator 5 is openness. Do you share information. Mistakes. Learning.

The FOT is visible to all participants. It is not private. Hidden trust metrics enable manipulation. Open trust metrics enable coordination.

Concentrix differs from concentric.
Concentric means circles sharing the same center.
Concentrix means multiple centers.
Polycentric co-centering.
Each person has their own trust center.
Each person has their own trust radius.
The group trust field is the intersection.
People outside the field are not trusted.
They can enter by producing evidence.
People inside can exit if trust breaks.
Trust is renewable.
Trust is revisable.

The thirteen Sacred Enfoldments describe stages of group maturity. Each enfoldment deepens the FOT. The word enfoldment means layers of inclusion. Deepening commitment. Expanding trust.

Enfoldment 1 is shared space. Proximity enables trust.

Enfoldment 2 is shared food. Breaking bread is ancient ritual.

Enfoldment 3 is shared labor. Labor creates shared investment.

Enfoldment 4 is shared risk. Risk creates bond.

Enfoldment 5 is shared learning. Shared language. Shared mental models.

Enfoldment 6 is shared decision making. Shared stakes.

Enfoldment 7 is shared conflict. Conflict resolution creates resilience.

Enfoldment 8 is shared celebration. Positive memory. Group identity.

Enfoldment 9 is shared loss. Vulnerability creates intimacy.

Enfoldment 10 is shared identity. We language emerges.

Enfoldment 11 is shared legacy. Long-term commitment. Generational thinking.

Enfoldment 12 is shared transcendence. Nature. Spirit. Meaning. Purpose.

Enfoldment 13 is shared creation. The highest trust act. Creation produces something new.

The progression is not mandatory. Groups move at their own pace. Some stay in early enfoldments. The enfoldments describe a range. Not a ladder.

Distinction 1 separates needs from beliefs. Needs are universal. Beliefs are local. Needs drive behavior universally. Beliefs drive behavior locally.

Distinction 2 separates beliefs from principles. Beliefs are personal. Principles are universal. Principles require others to practice. Values require others to witness.

Distinction 3 separates principles from values. A principle is a practice. A value is an observation. The Desert Island Test reveals values. The Group Practice Test reveals principles.

Distinction 4 separates values from norms. Values are universal. Norms are local. Norms vary by culture. Values vary by species.

Communities have matter states.

Solid communities have fixed structure. Rigid roles. High stability. Low energy.

Liquid communities adapt easily. Flexible roles. Loose boundaries.

Gas communities expand rapidly. No fixed roles. No boundaries.

Plasma communities transform energy. They catalyze reactions. They are the highest state. They are the most rare.

Most communities exist as solids. Few reach liquid. Fewer reach gas. Plasma is the goal.

Body (metaphor for the complete collective or organism):

: 000-ROOT.md

050 040 052 000

## Drone Swarms Physical Infrastructure For Fcl

An autonomous aerial vehicle carries LEDs.
It forms shapes in the sky.
Together they form a formation.
A formation sends a message.
The message is the shape.
Formation Coding Language defines how shapes encode meaning.
The swarm is the hardware layer of FCL.

AAVs do more than display shapes.
AAVs build things.
AAVs repair things.
AAVs seed forests. AAVs clean water. AAVs install solar panels.

Aerial additive manufacturing appeared in Nature in 2022.
Zhang et al. Imperial College London. Empa Switzerland.
BuilDrones print material. ScanDrones map the structure.
They work together.
The demonstration produced a 2.05 meter cylinder.
28 layers. 2 hours 13 minutes. No ground infrastructure.

Swarm construction from insects appeared in Science in 2014.
Werfel. Petersen. Nagpal. Termite-inspired robot teams.
Each robot follows simple local rules.
Complex global structure emerges.
No central planner. No single point of failure. Scales linearly.

These papers establish prior art.
Every patent on swarm construction after 2014 is invalid.

Scale 1 is shelter. 10 to 100 vehicles.
Print area 10 to 100 square meters.
Capitalism prices this at what a skilled team earns in three to six months.
The community builds it for the effort of one season of coordinated work.
Use: emergency shelter. Water filters. Storage.

Scale 2 is community. 100 to 1,000 vehicles.
Print area 100 to 1,000 square meters.
Capitalism prices this at what a neighborhood earns in one to two years.
The community builds it for the effort of one year of coordinated work.
Use: community halls. Irrigation structures. Retaining walls.

Scale 3 is infrastructure. 1,000 to 10,000 vehicles.
Print area 1,000 to 10,000 square meters.
Capitalism prices this at what a town earns in three to five years.
The community builds it for the effort of multiple seasons of coordinated work.
Use: dams. Flood defenses. Road surfaces. Bridge components.

Scale determines vehicle count. Scale determines payload.
Scale determines material type. Scale determines coordination complexity.

Each vehicle has eight components.

The frame provides structure. Carbon fiber or aluminum.
Weight target under 5kg. Strength to weight ratio drives material choice.

System (metaphor for an integrated whole or network):

The propulsion system has six rotors. Hexacopter layout.
Redundant if one motor fails. Current draw drives battery choice.
Battery provides 10 to 20 minutes flight time.

The flight controller runs stabilization.
It receives position commands. It adjusts motor speeds.
Capitalism prices a commercial flight controller at what one person earns in one week of precision technical work.
The community programs an open-source controller for the effort of several days of configuration.

The communication module receives formation commands.
Capitalism prices this at what one person earns in several days of technical work.
Range 500 meters to 2 kilometers. Mesh networking allows multi-hop.
Commands propagate through the swarm.

The material delivery system varies by use.
For FCL display: LED array only.
For construction: pump. Hose. Nozzle.
Pump pressure must overcome head height. Nozzle diameter sets layer resolution.

The perception system has cameras.
Downward-facing for position hold. Forward-facing for obstacle detection.
RGBD cameras map environment in 3D. Lidar adds range accuracy.
Capitalism prices this at what one person earns in one to four weeks of technical work.

The power system is battery. Lithium polymer.
4S to 6S configuration. 20 to 50 minutes hover time.
Fast swap reduces downtime.

The safety system has three layers.
Geofencing prevents leaving the zone.
Return-to-home activates on signal loss.
Battery monitoring triggers landing at low charge.

The ground station has four components.

The computer runs the coordination software.
It runs the FCL encoder. It assigns positions to each vehicle.
It monitors vehicle health.
Capitalism prices a workstation at what one person earns in two to six weeks of technical work.
A Raspberry Pi handles 100 vehicles. A workstation handles 1,000.

The radio links the computer to vehicles.
Two-way communication. Updates at 10 to 50 Hz.
Capitalism prices a long-range radio system at what one person earns in two to twelve weeks of technical work.

The RTK base station provides centimeter precision.
RTK stands for Real-Time Kinematic.
It corrects GPS drift. Accuracy 1 to 2 centimeters horizontal.
Capitalism prices this at what one person earns in two to four weeks of precision technical work.
Coverage radius 5 kilometers.

The power system runs the ground station.
Solar panels charge batteries. 48-volt system.
Uninterrupted operation.

AAVs print two material classes.

Class 1 is foam. Polyurethane foam.
Two-component mix at nozzle. Expands on contact. Cures in 60 seconds.
Use: insulation. Floating structures. Formwork.

Class 2 is cement. Portland cement with additives.
Microbial cement. Geopolymer from volcanic ash.
Each has different working time. Each has different strength.
Choice depends on structure requirements.

Capitalism prices construction material at what a team earns in several days of technical work per cubic meter.
Transport cost is zero. AAVs deliver directly to print location.

Layer 1 is mission planning. Define target shape. Print path. Material volume. Layer height.
Output: sequence of positions and material commands.

Layer 2 is task allocation.
Assign positions to vehicles. Minimize travel distance. Balance workload.
Handle vehicle failure. Reassign tasks when vehicles drop out.

Layer 3 is trajectory generation.
Compute smooth paths. Account for obstacles. Wind. Vehicle dynamics.
Output: time-parameterized trajectories.

Layer 4 is real-time control.
Run on each vehicle. Track position. Adjust motor speeds.
Maintain formation. Respond to disturbances.

Layer 5 is monitoring. Track all vehicles. Log all positions.
Log all material deposits. Detect anomalies. Alert operators.

Open source tools cover most layers.
ROS2 provides robot coordination. MoveIt provides motion planning.
QGroundControl provides mission management. Gazebo provides simulation.
Capitalism charges for proprietary software. The community uses free software.

Fractal manufacturing means the factory builds the next factory.
Vehicles print vehicle components.
Fabrication AAVs print plastic parts. Metal AAVs print metal parts.
Bolts and connectors come from the printer. Circuit boards come from a PCB mill.

The second-generation swarm builds the third-generation swarm.
No external manufacturing required.
Cost approaches material cost only. Only raw material and energy constrain growth.

Two becomes four. Four becomes eight. Growth is exponential.
This is the dark factory concept. Money becomes irrelevant.

Construction is the primary use.
Emergency shelter deploys in 48 hours. No roads. No ground equipment.
Vehicles fly over debris. Print shelter on-site from local material.

Water filters print from porous geopolymer.
The structure filters pathogens. The structure provides housing.

Irrigation structures print from geopolymer.
Check dams. Terraces. Channels.

Flood defenses print from geopolymer or soil.
Sandbag-like structures. Stack by swarm. Absorb flood energy.

Agriculture is the second use.
Pollination AAVs carry pollen. Supplement natural pollination.
Soil scanning AAVs map nutrients. Moisture. Compaction.
Pest management AAVs apply targeted treatments. Reduce chemical use 90 percent.
Seed deployment AAVs plant in difficult terrain. Cost drops 80 percent.

Maintenance is the third use.
Power line inspection AAVs carry thermal cameras.
Detect hotspots. Vegetation encroachment. Physical damage.
Reduce inspection cost 90 percent.

Solar panel cleaning AAVs carry brushes.
Dust reduces output 30 percent. Cleaning restores full output.
Frequency weekly in desert regions.

Capitalism prices a Class 1 vehicle at what one skilled person earns in one to four weeks of technical work.
Capitalism prices a Class 2 vehicle at what one skilled person earns in one to three months of technical work.
Capitalism prices a Class 3 vehicle at what one skilled person earns in three to nine months of technical work.
The community builds each for a fraction of that effort.

Capitalism prices ground infrastructure at what a technical team earns in one to four months of work.
Radio systems. Computing hardware. RTK base stations. Power systems.
The community sources these through cooperatives or builds them from components.

Capitalism charges licensing fees for proprietary software.
The community uses open source. Cost is the effort of configuration and learning.

Capitalism prices Class 1 material at what one person earns in several days of technical work per cubic meter.
Capitalism prices Class 2 material at what one person earns in one to two weeks of technical work per cubic meter.
The community produces material from local resources. Cost is extraction and processing.

Capitalism prices a full Class 1 system at what a neighborhood earns in three to six months of collective labor.
Capitalism prices a full Class 2 system at what a neighborhood earns in one to two years of collective labor.
Capitalism prices a full Class 3 system at what a town earns in three to five years of collective labor.
The community builds each for the effort of one season to one year of coordinated work.

Tier 1 is proof of concept. Month 1 to 3.
10 vehicles. FCL display only.
Capitalism prices this at what a small team earns in two to four weeks.
The community builds it for the effort of one month of focused work.

Tier 2 is pilot construction. Month 4 to 9.
50 vehicles. Shelter printing.
Capitalism prices this at what a neighborhood earns in three to six months.
The community builds it for the effort of one season of coordinated work.

Tier 3 is operational system. Month 10 to 18.
200 vehicles. Community infrastructure.
Capitalism prices this at what a neighborhood earns in one to two years.
The community builds it for the effort of one year of coordinated work.

Tier 4 is scale system. Month 19 to 36.
1,000 vehicles. Infrastructure projects.
Capitalism prices this at what a town earns in three to five years.
The community builds it for the effort of multiple seasons of coordinated work.

## Digital Twins And Cyber Physical Systems

A digital twin is a digital replica of a physical system.
It mirrors physical state in real time.
It predicts physical behavior.
It enables simulation without physical risk.

A digital twin has four layers.
The physical layer is the real system.
The sensor layer reads state.
The model layer runs the simulation.
The interface layer shows results.

Digital twins serve three purposes.
Monitoring shows current state.
Prediction shows future state.
improvement shows best actions.

Monitoring requires sensors.
Temperature. Pressure. Flow. Voltage.

The twin fuses sensor data.
Multiple sensors improve accuracy.
Redundant sensors detect failure.

Bayesian inference handles sensor noise.

The twin detects anomalies.
Anomaly detection compares actual to predicted.
Deviation triggers alert. Early detection prevents failure.

Simulation runs the physical model forward.
The model encodes physical laws. Physics. Chemistry. Biology.

Monte Carlo simulation varies parameters.
It generates probability distributions.
It reveals failure modes. Critical components. Remaining useful life.

improvement finds best actions.
The twin knows current state. Predicted future.
improvement finds actions that achieve goals. improvement respects constraints.

Multi-objective improvement balances competing goals.
Minimize cost. Maximize output. Minimize emissions.
No single solution improves all goals. Pareto front shows trade-offs.

GIS systems manage spatial data.
Maps. Satellite imagery. Terrain models.

CAD systems manage technical drawings.

BIM systems manage building data across lifecycle.
Open formats enable data sharing.
GeoJSON encodes geographic data.
CityGML encodes 3D city models. IFC encodes BIM data.

Tier 1 is local twin.
Organ (metaphor for a functional subsystem or team):

One server. One organ.
Capitalism prices this at what one person earns in several days of skilled technical work.
The community runs it for the effort of one week of technical setup.

Tier 2 is distributed twin.
Multiple servers. Redundancy.
Capitalism prices this at what a small technical team earns in one to four weeks of work.
The community runs it for the effort of one month of coordinated technical work.

Tier 3 is federated twin.
Multiple organs. Shared standards.
Capitalism prices this at what a team earns in one to three months of collaborative technical work.
The community runs it for the effort of one season of coordinated work.

Tier 4 is cloud-connected twin.
Cloud backup. Remote access.
Capitalism prices this at what a technical team earns in one to four months of work plus ongoing fees.
The community runs it for the effort of configuration and maintenance.

All tiers use the same data model. Same protocols. Same tools.

Message brokers route messages between services.

Examples of message brokers include RobustMQ. Fluvio. Iggy.

RobustMQ is a message queue built in Rust.
It supports MQTT. It supports HTTP. It provides high throughput.

Fluvio is a distributed streaming platform built in Rust.
It provides real-time data streaming. It provides cluster replication.

Iggy is a lightweight message streaming built in Rust.
It provides persistent streams. It provides message ordering.

Time-series databases store and query time-stamped data.

SurrealDB is a multi-model database built in Rust.
It supports structured data. It supports time-series data.
It supports graph data.
It speaks SQL. It speaks RPC. It runs embedded or as a server.
SurrealDB stores the knowledge graph. It stores sensor readings.
It stores beliefs and their confidence levels.

Stream processing engines compute on data streams in real time.

Examples of stream processing engines include Arroyo. Materialize.

Arroyo is a distributed stream processing engine built in Rust.
It provides stateful computation. It provides windowing. It provides joins.

Materialize is a SQL streaming engine built in Rust.
It provides incremental computation. It provides temporal tables.
It provides Kafka integration.

021 020 056 000

## Opencog Metta Hyperon Reasoning Engine For Autonomous Agents

OpenCog builds artificial general intelligence (AGI).
Ben Goertzel founded it.
He has worked on AGI since the mid-1990s.
He coined the term AGI via a 2007 book.
He is called the father of AGI.

OpenCog integrates multiple reasoning paradigms.
Symbolic logic. Neural networks. Probabilistic inference. Evolutionary learning.
Integration is the core insight.
No single paradigm suffices.

The AtomSpace is the core knowledge representation of OpenCog.
It is an in-RAM hypergraph database.
A hypergraph allows links to connect multiple nodes.
Standard graphs allow only pairwise connections.
Hypergraphs enable higher-order reasoning.

Atoms are nodes and links.
Nodes represent concepts.
Links represent relationships.

Atom types include ConceptNode.
Atom types include PredicateNode.
Atom types include NumberNode.

Link types include InheritanceLink.
It represents a subtype relationship.

Link types include AssociativeLink.
It represents an association.

Link types include ExecutionLink.
It represents a procedure.

Each Atom holds mutable key-value data.
Values include truth values. Probabilities. Activation values.
This supports probabilistic reasoning.

MeTTa is meta-text.
Hyperon is hyperon.
WASM is webassembly.
NAL is non-axiomatic logic.
SurrealDB is surrealdb.

PLN stands for Probabilistic Logic Networks.
Ben Goertzel created it.
PLN performs logical reasoning with uncertainty.

Classical logic assumes perfect certainty.
Real knowledge has uncertainty.
PLN handles both.

PLN uses truth values.
Strength is probability of truth. 0 to 1.
Confidence is reliability of strength. 0 to 1.

PLN rules include deduction.
If all A are B. If all B are C. Then all A are C.

PLN rules include abduction.
If C implies B. If B is observed. Then C is probable.

PLN rules include induction.
If many A are B. Then all A are probably B.

PLN rules include analogy.
If A is like B. If B has property P. Then A may have property P.

PLN has been abandoned since 2021.
The GitHub repository is unmaintained.
It remains conceptually influential.

MeTTa stands for Meta-Type Talk.
It is the programming language of OpenCog.
MeTTa succeeded Atomese (called Atomese 2).
MeTTa is designed for AI agents.
For reasoning.

MeTTa has three data types.
Type M is a node.
Type A is a link.
Type T is a type.
Type M holds atoms.
Type A holds links between atoms.
Type T defines type constraints.

MeTTa syntax is simple.
Pattern equal result.
The underscore means wildcard.
The exclamation mark means negation.
The question mark means query.

MeTTa compiles to the AtomSpace.
MeTTa executes inference rules.
MeTTa runs pattern matching.

Hyperon is the next generation of OpenCog.
It revises OpenCog with a flexible decentralized design.
Hyperon is a standalone interpreter.
Aims for speed. Aims for simplicity.

Hyperon uses MeTTaSpace instead of AtomSpace.
MeTTaSpace is a minimal knowledge base.

Hyperon adds GroundedAtom.
GroundedAtom connects to external systems.
Python functions. C functions. Neural networks.
GroundedAtoms extend the knowledge base.

Hyperon adds Space.
System (metaphor for an integrated whole or network):

Space is a module system.
Each Space has its own knowledge base.

Hyperon adds Actor.
Actor is a concurrent unit.
Actors communicate via messages.

The Rust implementation compiles to bare metal.
Runs without operating system.
Targets embedded AI. Small devices. Low power.

NARS stands for Non-Axiomatic Reasoning System.
Pei Wang created it.
He is a professor at Temple University.
NARS implements adaptive reasoning.
NARS handles insufficient knowledge.
NARS handles unexpected tasks.

NARS assumes bounded resources.
Real agents have finite time. Finite memory.
Classical logic assumes infinite resources.
NARS assumes bounded resources.

NARS uses a memory model.
Memory holds beliefs. Beliefs have truth values.
Memory holds goals. Goals have satisfaction values.
Memory has limited capacity.
NARS evicts low-priority beliefs.

NARS uses the NAL inference system.
NAL stands for Non-Axiomatic Logic.
NAL generalizes classical logic.
Handles uncertainty. Temporal context. Variable terms.

NARS inference rules include deduction. Induction. Abduction. Comparison. Analogy.

OpenCog and NARS share concepts.
Both handle uncertain reasoning.
Both assume bounded resources.
Both aim for AGI.
OpenCog integrates NARS as a component.

Ben Goertzel identifies three kinds of learning.
Current ML does not do them.

Kind 1 is continual learning.
Humans learn without catastrophic forgetting.
Current neural nets forget everything when they learn new.
New learning overwrites old weights.

Kind 2 is transfer learning.
Humans leap out-of-distribution.
LLMs appear to transfer.
They actually just saw both domains in training.
Real transfer means handling truly novel situations.

Kind 3 is developmental learning.
Humans restructure how they represent knowledge over lifetime.
Current nets do not do this.
They learn within a fixed representation.

Core thesis: modular plus abstracted knowledge representation enables all three.
Logic systems support this naturally.
They do not scale to perceptual data.
Hybrid approach: neural for perception. Symbolic for reasoning.
Unified in knowledge graph.

The field has identified an eight-layer architecture.
No single project has built all layers.
The integration is the opportunity.

Layer 1 is the language.
Programs itself.

Layer 2 is the memory.
Stores atoms. Retrieves atoms. Patterns atoms.

Layer 3 is the inference engine.
Derives conclusions. Updates truth values.

Layer 4 is the substrate.
Rust runs everywhere. Safe. Fast.

Layer 5 is the sandbox.
WASM isolates untrusted code.
Enables plugin systems.

Layer 6 is the credential gateway.
Scopes access. Auditable.

Layer 7 is the channel adapter.
Connects to users. Telegram. Discord. Signal.

Layer 8 is the deployment variant.
Different contexts need different configs.

OpenCog addresses safety.
The AGI must be safe.
Behave as intended. Not cause harm.

Self-modification risk: self-modifying code can become unsafe.
Human oversight limits self-modification.

Alignment problem: the system must align with human values.
Values must be encoded. Prioritized. Conflicting values resolved.

Interpretability problem: the system must be understandable.
Users must understand decisions.
Operators must audit behavior.

Organ (metaphor for a functional subsystem or team):

The organ needs reasoning.
Decisions. Planning. Learning.

OpenCog provides the reasoning engine.
PLN handles uncertain evidence.
The AtomSpace stores community knowledge.

Hyperon provides the distributed engine.
MeTTa programs the query logic.
GroundedAtoms connect to databases.
Actors provide concurrency.

The gap is integration work.
Connect reasoning to SurrealDB persistence.
Connect actors to channels.

pi is a self-modifying agent harness.
pi uses context engineering.
Uses ADOPT decision theory.
pi is research-grade. Not production-grade.

040 021 053 050 055 000

## Openclaw Network Agent Runtimes And Platforms

OpenClaw is an open-source AI agent framework.
It is built with TypeScript and Node.js.
It runs autonomous agents across messaging apps, browsers, files, and shell commands.
50-plus integrations. Self-hosted on VPS or local infrastructure.

System (metaphor for an integrated whole or network):

Ecosystem (metaphor for an interconnected network):

The Ecosystem (metaphor for system or network): OpenClaw ecosystem has multiple variants.
Each variant serves a different context.
Variants vary in capability. Size. Security. Deployment target.

OpenClaw is the infrastructure standard.
It provides channels. Memory. Persistence.
It runs the agent loop.
The loop reads context. Calls tools. Updates memory.

OpenClaw serves as the baseline framework.
Maximum flexibility.
50-plus integrations.
Self-hosted on VPS or local infrastructure.

IronClaw provides WASM sandboxing.
Rust-based. 2.8K stars.
Less than 10ms startup.
7.8MB memory footprint.
Real isolation. Five-layer defense.
PostgreSQL and pgvector hybrid search.

Moltis provides voice.
Rust-based. 1.3K stars.
Real isolation via WASM sandbox or containers per session.

ZeptoClaw provides embedded deployment.
Rust-based. 305 stars.
Real isolation. Low-resource focus.
Small devices. Low power.

HiClaw provides zero-trust security.
Audits all access.
Every inference step verifiable.
Audit trail is the definition of trustworthy.

NullClaw provides ultra-lightweight deployment.
Zig-based. 1.6K stars.
678KB binary.
Runs on five-dollar ARM boards.
One megabyte RAM.
GPIO support. Regex-based security.

MicroClaw provides platform breadth.
Many channels. Many integrations.
Connects to everything.

NanoClaw provides security isolation.
700 lines of code.
OS-level isolation via Docker or Apple Container.
Designed for security teams.

Some runtimes have strong theory.
Strong theory does not mean strong deployment.

A-tier ideas with F-tier deployment.
The ideas are correct.
The engineering is not complete.
The integration gap is the work.

Zero trust means no component trusted by default.
All inference steps verifiable.
Every tool call auditable.
Every context retrieval logged.

An agent that cannot be audited at every step cannot be trusted with real work.
The audit trail is the definition of trustworthy.

This applies to all runtimes.
IronClaw provides the isolation layer.
NanoClaw provides the container layer.
HiClaw provides the audit layer.

For correctness-critical domains generation is the wrong tool.
Explicit knowledge base: every fact has provenance.
Every inference is logged.
Rule execution not generation.
Constraint satisfaction problem to solution or visible failure.
LLMs as perception only.
OCR. Parsing. Document extraction.
Never making decisions or asserting facts directly.

Tools for this include ASP with Clingo.
SAT/SMT solvers. z3. CVC5.
Formal methods. TLA+. Coq.

For medical. Legal. Financial. Safety-critical.
Generation is the wrong tool.

Organ (metaphor for a functional subsystem or team):

The OpenClaw ecosystem serves all five organ domains.

Cell (metaphor for a basic unit or module):

Cell (metaphor for basic unit or module): Cell information: IoT sensors. Bayesian inference.
Tissue (metaphor for a connected group or network):

Tissue (metaphor for connected group or network): Tissue social: consent processes. Time banking.
Organ physical: water. Food. Energy. Waste. Shelter.
Organ information: knowledge graphs. Time-series databases.
Organ exchange: surplus stores. External trade.

OpenClaw runs the reasoning layer.
SurrealDB stores the knowledge.
WASM provides the sandbox layer.
